coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient photocatalysis toward tetracycline of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots sensitized bismuth tungstate based on interfacial charge transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jin
Yuan, Xingzhong
Jiang, Longbo
Wu, Zhibin
Chen, Xiaohong
Wang, Hou
Wang, Hui
Zeng, Guangming
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 was synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method. Multiple techniques were applied to investigate the structures, morphologies, optical and electronic properties and photocatalytic performance of as-prepared samples. The results indicated that the hybrid materials were formed with N-CQDs attached on the surface of sphere-like Bi2WO6. The photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials was evaluated sufficiently by using tetracycline (TC) as target organic pollutant. N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 displayed superior photocatalytic efficiency with nearly 97% removal of TC in 25min and 86.37% mineralization in 90min. The degradation reaction coefficient (kobs) was approximately two times higher than pure Bi2WO6 as a result of the synergistic effects of N-CQDs and Bi2WO6. Increased light harvesting capacity, excellent electron transfer ability and improved molecular oxygen activation ability were obtained in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials caused by N-CQDs. The present study demonstrated that N-CQDs modification was an effective way to improve photocatalytic efficiency, which can be extended to a general strategy for other semiconductors. The study indicated that novel N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 has a great potential for rapid and efficient treatment of organic pollutants in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi2WO6
Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Visible light photocatalysis
Tetracycline

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and compositions of the as-prepared photocatalysts
Morphology and textural property of the as-prepared photocatalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of the as-prepared pure Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials.
Raman spectroscopy of as-prepared pure Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials.
XPS spectra of the pure Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials. (a) Survey of the sample; (b) Bi 4f; (c) W 4f; (d) O 1s; (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s.

SEM and TEM images of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials. (a) The low magnification SEM image; (b) high magnification SEM image; (c) low magnification TEM image and (d–e) high magnification TEM image.
Typical SEM image of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 materials and corresponding elemental mapping images of Bi, W, O, C and N.
EDS image of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 and the percentage of elements in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5. (The carbon content is inaccurate in EDS analysis due to the existence of carbon membrane.)
PL spectra of pure- Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials.
UV−vis spectra of the as-prepared pure Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials.

(a) Transient photocurrent response of pure Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 and CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials under visible light irradiation. (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 and CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of TC in the presence of pure Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6, CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials and photolysis of TC under visible light irradiation and (b) kinetic fit for the degradation of TC with the pure Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials.
TC degradation of Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials under the monochromatic light irradiation at wavelength 365, 578, 630, and 940nm for 3h, respectively.

(a) Recyclability of the photocatalytic decomposition of TC by N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials; (b) XRD patterns of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials before and after the cycling photocatalytic experiments.
Trapping experiment of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TC over N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid material under visible light irradiation.
ESR spectra of radical adducts trapped by DMPO in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials: (a) methanol dispersion and (b) aqueous dispersion under visible light irradiation.
Schematic diagram of the charge transfer in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 combined with the possible reaction mechanisms of photocatalysis under solar light.
TOC analysis of TC (10 mg/L) degradation by Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 under visible irradiation for different time periods.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient photocatalysis toward tetracycline of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots sensitized bismuth tungstate based on interfacial charge transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control, Hunan University, Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Mobile E-business 2011 Collaborative Innovation Center of Hunan Province

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 was synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method. Multiple techniques were applied to investigate the structures, morphologies, optical and electronic properties and photocatalytic performance of as-prepared samples. The results indicated that the hybrid materials were formed with N-CQDs attached on the surface of sphere-like Bi2WO6. The photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials was evaluated sufficiently by using tetracycline (TC) as target organic pollutant. N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 displayed superior photocatalytic efficiency with nearly 97% removal of TC in 25min and 86.37% mineralization in 90min. The degradation reaction coefficient (kobs) was approximately two times higher than pure Bi2WO6 as a result of the synergistic effects of N-CQDs and Bi2WO6. Increased light harvesting capacity, excellent electron transfer ability and improved molecular oxygen activation ability were obtained in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials caused by N-CQDs. The present study demonstrated that N-CQDs modification was an effective way to improve photocatalytic efficiency, which can be extended to a general strategy for other semiconductors. The study indicated that novel N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 has a great potential for rapid and efficient treatment of organic pollutants in water.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, solar energy conversion and pollutant removal have attracted considerable attention because of energy crisis and environmental pollution [1–3]. Obviously, semiconductor-based photocatalyst was regarded as one of the significant role for solving these issues [4–6]. However, it is a rigorous challenge to maximize the efficiency of capture and conversion of solar energy for photocatalyst. In recent years, many robust approaches are desirable to design novel photocatalytic materials for utilizing solar energy to degrade pollutant efficiently [7–11].

Bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6) has aroused much attention among the reported visible-light photocatalysts, due to its suitable band gaps, relatively high photostability, superior band composition, unique crystal structure and nontoxicity [12–14]. However, the photocatalytic activity of pure Bi2WO6 is always limited by its low efficiency of light absorption, difficult migration and high recombination probability of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. In order to overcome the above drawbacks and further improve the photocatalytic performance of Bi2WO6, many methods have been tried. Firstly, the strategy is based on the ion doping of Bi2WO6, which could change the surface properties and extend the light responsive range [1,15]. Secondly, coupling different semiconductor materials with Bi2WO6 is also an effective way to improve the photocatalytic activity [16,17]. Unfortunately, the traditional methods for the modification of Bi2WO6 are impact and insufficient, due to the large-size materials cannot construct perfect interfaces. Therefore, the surface defects may appear through the formation of interfaces in the hybrid structures, and the electron-hole pairs may tend to recombine on surface defects and then limit the photocatalytic performance. To overcome the above shortages, small size quantum dots materials can be used due to the uniform distribution and the formation of sufficient contraction [18,19].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the size less than 10nm have gradually become a research hotspot [20–22]. It possess an amorphous nanocrystalline core with predominantly sp2 conjugated structure and contain certain oxygen content in the form of various oxygen-containing species such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Hence, the CQDs show excellent electron transfer and reservoir properties, which can be employed as a co-catalyst to build surface separation centers for electrons and reduce the recombination rate of charge carriers CQDs [23,24]. Recently, CQDs has been introduced into photocatalysis field to improve the photocatalytic activity for pollutant removal, such as CQDs/BiOX (X=Br, Cl) [25], CQDs/Ag/Ag2O [26], CQDs/ZnO [27], CQDs/In2S3 [28], CQDs/TiO2 [29]. Actually, it was still highly desirable to further improve the performance of CQDs by appropriate strategies to acquire satisfactory high-efficiently photocatalytic activity of CQDs-based materials. Moreover, nitrogen doping can bring the unique physical and chemical properties of CQDs. Due to the fact that nitrogen doping can efficiently induce charge delocalization, lower the work function and effectively enhance the photoluminescence emission performance of CQDs. In addition, the electron transfer capability of carbon materials can be also efficiently promoted [30–32]. Thus, if N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) was built and modified on semiconductors, the superior photocatalytic activity was expected.

Herein, the novel N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 was synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method for the first time. The structures, morphologies and optical properties of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials are investigated in detail. Antibiotic tetracycline (TC) was selected as the target pollutant and the photocatalytic activities of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials are evaluated. The mechanism of pollutant photodegradation in this reaction system is studied by using free radicals trapping experiments and ESR analysis. It possess both excellent interfacial charge transfer and increased light harvesting capacity so as to enhance the photocatalytic activity.

Ammonium citrate, ethylenediamine and HNO3 (65%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). TC, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and Na2WO4·2H2O were purchased from Jiangtian Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All the chemicals were used without further purification.

The N-CQDs aqueous solution was synthesized according to the literature [31]. 5mmol ammonium citrate was dissolved in 10mL deionized water and 335 μL ethylenediamine was added. After being stirred for 30min, the solution was then transferred to 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heat treating for 5h at 200°C. Subsequently, the reactor was cooled down naturally. Eventually, the obtained product was subjected to dialysis (MWCO 1000) for 24h in order to obtain the N-CQDs solution. The CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method [33].

N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. In typical procedure, 1mmolBi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in a 20 mL aqueous solution containing 1mmol of nitric acid to form a clear solution and defined as solution A. Then 0.5mmol Na2WO4·2H2O was dissolved in a certain amount of N-CQDs aqueous solution and the solution was topped up to 20 mL by deionized water to obtain solution B. Solution B was added into solution A dropwise under stirring and stirred for 2h. Then the suspension was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160°C for 20h. Subsequently, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The samples were washed and dried in an oven at 60°C overnight. According to the addition of 0, 1, 3, 5 and 8 mL N-CQDs aqueous solution, the obtained N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 were named as pure Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-1, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-3, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-8, respectively.

The crystal structure of the as-prepared samples were characterized by an X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) using Ni-filtered CuKα radiation in the 2θ ranging from 5 to 90° at a scan rate of 0.02°s−1 . Raman spectra were obtained using the Horiba Jobin Yvon LabRAM HR800 at room temperature with a 532nm solid laser as the exciting source. X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to identify surface chemical composition and chemical states of the catalysts on ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were taken on a FEI-quanta 200F scanning electron microscope equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) operated at an acceleration voltage of 20kV. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) and High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were taken on a JEOL JEM-2100F (200kV) transmission electron microscope. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared samples were recorded by UV2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) using BaSO4 as a reflectance standard. The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) experiments were performed using an electrochemical station (CHI660D, Shanghai Chenhua Ltd., China) in a conventional three-electrode configuration. The electrolyte was 0.01M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with an Edinburgh FLsp920 transient fluorescence spectrometer. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of radicals spin-trapped were operated on a Bruker model ESR E500 spectrometer by spin-trap reagent DMPO (Sigma Chemical Co.) in methanol and water, respectively.

Photocatalytic activities of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials were evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) under visible light. A 300W Xe lamp (Perfect Light, wavelength range: 320nm≤λ≤780nm, light intensity: 160mW/cm2) was used as the light source with a 420nm cutoff filter to provide visible-light irradiation. The monochromatic light lamps with the wavelength of 365, 578, 630 and 940nm. In a typical procedure, 100 mL of TC (10 mg/L) and 0.05g of photocatalysts were added in a 250ml beaker. Before illumination, the suspension was constantly stirred for 60min in dark until the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was established between TC and photocatalysts. At 5min intervals, a 5mL solution was collected and centrifuged at 8000rpm for 5min to remove the photocatalysts. Then the concentration of TC was detected by recording the absorbance at the characteristic band of 357nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–2550, SHIMADZU, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and compositions of the as-prepared photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structures of the pure Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 with different contents of N-CQDs were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and the results was showed in Fig. 1 . The distinct diffraction peaks of samples can be found at 28.3°, 32.8°, 47.1°, 55.8°/56.0°, 58.5°, 68.8°, 75.9°,78.2°/78.3° and 87.5°, which can be indexed to (131), (200), (202), (331)/(133), (262), (400), (193), (460)/(402) and (462) crystal planes of Bi2WO6 (JCPDS NO.39-0256), respectively. The typical diffaction peaks of N-CQDs cannot be detected at 26°, which caused by low content, low crystallinity and high dispersion of N-CQDs in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials. It also suggests that the N-CQDs have no obvious influence on the crystal structure of Bi2WO6. The similar results can also be found in previous reports [34,35].

To determine the surface microstructural difference of the as-prepared pure Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials, the Raman investigation was carried out and the results was showed in Fig. 2 . For Bi2WO6, several peaks in the range of 600–1000 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum are assigned to the stretching of the W-O band. In detail, the peaks at 795 and 827 cm−1 are attributed to antisymmetric and symmetric Ag modes of the O-W-O group terminus, respectively. The peak at 308 cm−1 is assigned to the translation modes involving simultaneous motions of Bi3+ and WO6 6− [15]. The intensity of the peak at 727 cm−1 is interpreted as an antisymmetric bridging mode associated with the tungstate chain [36]. For N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5, the d-band and G-band of carbon at 1351 and 1592 cm−1 can be seen [37], which implies the existence of N-CQDs in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5.

To analyze the chemical composition and the interaction between Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs, XPS spectra of Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials were recorded. The survey spectrum was shown in Fig. 3 a and indicated that the as-prepared Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 mainly contain bismuth, tungsten, oxygen, carbon and nitrogen elements. In Fig. 3b, the results of Bi2WO6 sample showed that binding energy at 159.4eV for Bi 4f 7/2 and 164.6eV for Bi 4f 5/2, respectively, which meaning that the Bi element exists in the chemical state of Bi3+. The Bi 4f peaks in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 showed a slight shift when compare with the Bi 4f peaks in the Bi2WO6 sample. This indicates the existence of interaction between the Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs, which may be referred to the presence of BiC bond in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials [35,38]. The high-resolution W 4f XPS spectrum of Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 samples were shown in Fig. 3c. The binding energies of W 4f 5/2 and W 4f 7/2 are 37.9eV and 35.7eV in the oxide form of Bi2WO6, which can attributed to the W element exists in the chemical state of W6+ [1]. The O 1s peaks at 530.4eV (Fig. 3d) can be assigned to the oxygen in Bi2WO6 crystals [35]. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) can be fitted into three peaks located at 284.5, 285.1, and 286.0eV, which were assigned to CC/C C, CN, and CO, respectively [39,40]. The peak in N 1s spectrum was located at 401.1, which was attributed to the NH bonds (Fig. 3f) [37]. The XPS analysis indicated the N-CQDs have been modified on the Bi2WO6 materials and intimate integration has been achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and textural property of the as-prepared photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The elaborate structure of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 was further revealed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It can be observed from Fig. 4c and d that a large number of dark dots with diameters of about 8nm, implying that the N-CQDs have been anchored on the surface of Bi2WO6 successfully and intimate integration. The results provided a convincing evidence to explain the formation of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6. Fig. 4e shows the HRTEM image of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials. The lattice spacing of N-CQDs is determined to be 0.332nm, which corresponding to the (002) crystal plane [41]. The lattice fringe spacing of some N-CQDs cannot be observed and this is due to that N-CQDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline cores. At the same time, the lattice spacing around 0.328nm can be found, which agree well with the (131) crystallographic plane of Bi2WO6 (JCPDS NO.39-0256) [42]. The above results further demonstrate that the N-CQDs have modified on the Bi2WO6 to construct N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials.

The efficiency of charge separation has a vital influence on performance of photocatalytic process. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra is a technology which have been widely used to study the charge transfer, migration and recombination processes in photocatalysts. From Fig. 7 , the pure Bi2WO6 displayed a strong PL intensity, signifying the high recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in pure Bi2WO6 materials. While the PL intensity of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials markedly decreased compare with the pure Bi2WO6, which meaning that the N-CQDs anchored on the Bi2WO6 surface has severely restrained the recombination of photogenerated charge electron-hole pairs. This phenomenon indicating that, due to the N-CQDs possesses the conjugated p structure, introduce N-CQDs could facilitate the charges transfer from Bi2WO6 to N-CQDs and enabled the effective separation of charge carriers. Moreover, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups on the surface of N-CQDs could induce the high upward band bending, which will also reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs [43,44].

To study the function of N-CQDs, the optical properties of the pure Bi2WO6 and the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials were analyzed via the UV−vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS), and the result is shown in Fig. 8 . The pure Bi2WO6 displayed the absorption ranging from 200nm to 450nm, which stemed from the electronic transition from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of Bi2WO6 upon excitation by light. It implied that Bi2WO6 was less responsive to visible light [1]. It can be seen that there apparently enhanced absorption intensity in the region ranging from 200 to 700nm with the increases contents of N-CQDs, which means that introduced N-CQDs have significantly increased the optical properties of light absorption for N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials. It is ascribing to the N-CQDs could serve as a photosensitizer and work as photocenter for absorbing visible light. Moreover, the interfacial charge transfer effect also contribute to this phenomenon [45,46]. The enhanced light absorption may promote the formation of electron-hole pairs and thus leads to an enhanced photocatalytic activity [47]. However, excessive amounts of the N-CQDs on the surface of Bi2WO6 trended to accumulate together to form lager particles (>10nm), resulting in decreased the optical properties of light absorption for N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 [48]. This is the possible reason for that N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-8 possesses lower absorption intensity in visible light.

The transient photocurrent response curves of the as-prepared pure Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 and CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials were measured, and the results are showed in Fig. 9 a. Under visible light irradiation, the photocurrent intensity for N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials was much higher than that of pure Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6, suggesting a longer lifetime of electron–hole pair photogenerated over N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials than the pure Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6. Normally, the photogenerated electrons and holes would recombine quickly and lead to a relative low photocurrent intensity. When the N-CQDs was modified on the surface of Bi2WO6 sphere-like structure to build intimate integration, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of Bi2WO6 could transfer to N-CQDs, and thus result in the effective separation of photogenerated charge carriers and prolong the lifetime of the electron-hole pairs [49]. Moreover, the nitrogen doping can efficiently induce charge delocalization and lower the work function of CQDs which leads to high photocurrent intensity.

To get further insight into electron-transport and charge separation properties of the pure Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) analysis was carried out, and the result is shown in Fig. 9b. It can be seen clearly that N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials showed smaller semicircle at high frequencies when compared with pure Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials, implying that the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials possess much lower resistance and faster transport of charge carriers can be achieved over the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 electrode. The highly conductive N-CQDs with intimate contaction of Bi2WO6 can facilitate electron transfer from Bi2WO6 within the whole electrode and thus decrease resistance.

In order to study the photocatalytic activities of pure Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 and CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials under the visible light irradiation, the tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) was employed as the targeted pollutant. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the direct photolysis of TC can be neglected during the whole visible-light irradiation, indicating that TC is a stable pollutant. The N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials exhibited better photocatalytic performance than pure Bi2WO6 for TC degradation. After the visible-light irradiation for 25min, 97.0% of TC can be photodegraded by N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5while only 77.9% of TC can be degraded by pure Bi2WO6, suggesting the introduction of N-CQDs was an efficient strategy for the improvement of photocatalytic activity. For the CQDs/Bi2WO6-5, only 83% of TC can be degraded which means that the CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 showed lower photocatalytic degradation efficiency than the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5.

The kinetics of TC degradation under visible light irradiation was also investigated (Fig. 10b). The apparent rate constants for Bi2WO6, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-1, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-3, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-8 and CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 are 0.06242, 0.08391, 0.10684, 0.13942, 0.07252 and 0.0754min−1, respectively. The photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials exhibited approximately two times higher than that of pure Bi2WO6.

The N-CQDs could absorb long wavelength light and emit shorter wavelength light, which is expected to be applied in photocatalysis [31]. To prove this, we then carefully studied the photocatalytic activity of Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials under different monochromatic light (365, 578, 630, and 940nm). As shown in Fig. 11 , the photocatalysis of TC could be ignored under light irradiation. The photocatalytic efficiency of Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials both enhanced with the decrease of the wavelength, which means that Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials hold the property of up-conversion. Furthermore, with the decrease of the wavelength, the photocatalytic efficiency of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials increased more quickly compared with the photocatalytic efficiency of Bi2WO6. Under 365, 578, 630 and 940nm light irradiation, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials showed efficient TC degradation of 87.5%, 54.2%, 20.8 and 6.6%, respectively. Bi2WO6 showed efficient TC degradation of 44.9%, 22.4%, 14.4% and 3.4%, respectively. The results well demonstrated that the introduced N-CQDs can improve the harvesting capacity of Bi2WO6 and more electrons-hole pairs could be generated on N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 hybrid materials. Accordingly, the electrons localized on oxygen vacancies may also increase, which subsequently promoted the formation of
O2− by the molecular oxygen activation with oxygen vacancies. Therefore, the up-conversion effect of N-CQDs could efficiently improve the generation number of
O2− on Bi2WO6 with oxygen vacancies.

The mineralization ability is also an essential parameter to evaluate the photocatalytic properties of the photocatalysts [50]. Table 1 presented the total organic carbon (TOC) removal by Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5. After 90min photoreaction, 86.37% of TOC was removed in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 reaction system, which was much higher than that of pure Bi2WO6 (15.18%). Hence, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 exhibited a very desirable mineralization capacity to TC and promising application potential for wastewater treatment.

As the stability of photocatalysts is a general requirement for the practical application, the reusability of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 composite was investigated. As shown in Fig. 12 a, after five recycles, no obvious loss of photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 materials can be observed, revealing the high stability of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 materials. On the basis of XRD analysis of used sample and fresh sample (Fig. 12b), it can be seen that there is no obvious change in the crystalline phase of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 material after the photocatalytic process, which is in line with its remarkable repeated photoactivity. These results demonstrate that the photocatalysts remained stable and active for repeated usage.

To investigate the mechanism of the photocatalytic process, the active species trapping experiments were performed to determine the active species during the photodegradation process of TC (Fig. 13 ). The experiments involve the utilization of 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), triethanolamine (TEAO), and isopropanol (IPA) as the scavengers for superoxide radicals (
O2−), holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (
OH), respectively. The photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 was nearly invariable when IPA (quencher of
OH) was added, indicating that hydroxyl radicals (
OH) does not contribute to the degradation of TC. On the contrary, when the BQ and TEAO were added into reaction solution, the photodegradation of TC was significantly inhibited, respectively. Thus, the superoxide (
O2−) and holes (h+) are the major reactive species in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 reaction system.

To gain further evaluate the mechanism of degradation by the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 materials, the ESR spin-trap tests with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were performed, and the results are shown in Fig. 14 . There are no ESR signals were detected when the reaction is carried out in the dark. Under visible light irradiation, the six characteristic peaks of the DMPO-
O2− species and four characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH can be observed, indicating that both the
O2− and
OH radicals produced in N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 reaction systems. It also can be seen that the intensities of the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2− and DMPO-
OH increased with irradiation time. The photogenerated electrons on the CB of Bi2WO6 (−1.02eV) could react with the adsorbed O2 to form
O2− due to the position of Bi2WO6 is more negative than the potential of O2/
O2− (−0.33V vs. NHE) [51,52]. What’s more, N-CQDs trap the photogenerated electrons and promote the formation of
O2−, which derived from the oxygen defects on the surface of N-CQDs [53,54]. Usually,
OH are generated by the way of holes oxidizing H2O. Whereas, the VB of Bi2WO6 (1.73eV) is lower than the standard redox potential of
OH, H+/H2O (2.72V vs. NHE) [51,52], which implies that the photoexcited holes in the VB of Bi2WO6 cannot oxidize the adsorbed H2O molecules to product
OH. Therefore, the
OH may be generated by further reduction of
O2−. The photoelectrons photoelectrons of Bi2WO6 transferred to N-CQDs, can via a one or two-electron reduction process reduce O2 to H2O2 or
O2−, respectively (O2 +e− →
O2−;
O2− +e− +2H+ →H2O2). The generation of
O2− and H2O2 can be further converted
OH radicals by the subsequent reaction (H2O2 →2
OH;
O2− +H2O→
OH+OH− +O2) [26]. Therefore, according to the above results of the active species trapping experiments and this ESR analysis, it can be inferred that the
O2− played a major role in the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 photocatalytic reactions rather than the
OH.

Based on the above results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism diagram of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 15 . First of all, due to the introduction of N-CQDs to the Bi2WO6, the light harvesting ability of the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials increased significantly, which revealing that N-CQDs could work as photocenter for absorbing visible light. The following is that, when the visible light was irradiated on the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials, the electrons on the VB of Bi2WO6 was excited to the CB and leaving the holes on the VB of Bi2WO6. Normally, these photogenerated electrons and holes quickly recombine and only a portion of charges could participate in the photocatalytic process. However, the N-CQDs possesses excellent electron transfer and collection ability, the electrons on the CB of Bi2WO6 tend to transfer to N-CQDs and enable the spatial separation of the photogenerated charge carriers, result in an enhanced photocatalytic activity. Hence, the N-CQDs could act as charge separation center. Then, the electrons on the N-CQDs tended to capture the reaction and the process, which could activate the O2 in the solution to form
O2− via single electron reduction. Noteworthily, a large population of diverse surface defect sites can be found on the surface of N-CQDs [31,53,54], which made the molecular oxygen could be easier activated via the N-CQDs in this system. Theoretically, the generated
O2− would play vital role in the photodegradation process, leading to dramatic photocatalytic performance. As a result, the synergetic effect between Bi2WO6 and N-CQDs leads to the enhancement of photodegradation activity for TC.

Novel N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials with different N-CQDs contents have been synthesized for the first time through a facile hydrothermal method. The N-CQDs were anchored on the surface of Bi2WO6 and the chemical bonds were formed. As a result of the modification of N-CQDs, the photocatalytic activity of Bi2WO6 for the degradation of TC increased dramatically under visible light irradiation. N-CQDs/Bi2WO6-5 displayed superior photocatalytic efficiency with nearly 97% removal of TC (10 mg/L) in 25min and 86.37% mineralization in 90min. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials was attributed to the increased light harvesting capacity, excellent electron transfer ability, and improved molecular oxygen activation ability of N-CQDs. In addition, the N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 materials possess excellent reusability and stability, which was valuable for the potential applications of environmental remediation. Hopefully, this facile hydrothermal synthesis strategy could be extended to fabricate other N-CQDs-semiconductor hybridizations to obtain novel multifunctional materials and properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the Key Research and Development Project of Hunan Province, China (No. 2016SK2015), Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51521006), and Key Project of National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 71431006).
